Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé
Teacher: Emma Saturday
Level: Emma 2024/2025

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good
Comment:

Chloé participates enthusiastically in all the activities. She enjoys story time and can repeat the story with her classmates and her teacher. Chloé also shows a real interest in the worksheet activities, the "what's missing" activity, and the new story tattoo activity. She is always cooperative with her teacher and her classmates.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good
Comment:

Chloé's English knowledge is good. She knows a great amount of vocabulary and structures. She can often use them in sentences, sometimes requiring the teacher's help to do so. This term, she specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place (e.g.: sleep in the bedroom, eat in the kitchen, etc.). She has also reinforced her knowledge of the shapes, the colours, the moods, the body parts and the food items. Chloé can pair up Emma's clean/dirty body parts, repeat the right structures, and place tattoos on the correct body parts with a little help. She can also tell her age and gender using the complete structure.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good
Comment:

Chloé is a joyful and attentive student. She treats her classmates and her teacher with respect and care. She listens attentively in class and usually stays focused during the lesson, though she can be distracted at times. On a few occasions, she asked repetitive questions, which annoyed her classmates, but she stopped when reminded. Chloé takes good care of the material and shares it with her classmates.

Final comment

Throughout the school year, Chloé has shown great enthusiasm and cooperation in class. She has made significant progress in her English learning, developing both her memory and her comprehension skills. It is recommended to reinforce the vocabulary and structures learnt throughout the school year to ensure continued improvement.

componentResults.reason: The report follows the required structure with Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour, and Final comment, and each main section includes an assessment and comment. It synthesises multiple observations into a cohesive, warm narrative and avoids invented marks, absences, audio frequency, or teacher names. Most claims are grounded in the notes, especially regarding colours/clothes, age/gender structures, worksheets, traffic light reminders, tiredness/distraction, story help, and hands-on activities. However, a few statements are only weakly traceable or slightly invented, such as sharing materials, collaborating well, treating everyone with respect, and having 'grown more comfortable.' The story comment also under-synthesises conflicting notes, since one note says she knew the story very well while another says she did not know it. Overall it is a strong report with minor grounding issues.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Class: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé usually comes to class in a variable mood, sometimes tired or distracted, but she can also be in a very good mood and eager to participate. She shows willingness to learn, especially when activities involve repetition or familiar structures, such as telling her age and gender, which she does almost always using the complete structure. Chloé enjoys interactive activities like the "what's missing" game and dressing Emma, where she actively engages with the material. However, she occasionally holds back when new vocabulary or concepts are introduced, such as colours or clothing items, requiring more guidance than her peers. Chloé is generally cooperative with her classmates and teacher, though she sometimes needs reminders to focus, particularly when asking repetitive questions or when the group is playful and loud.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:

Chloé’s English knowledge is developing, though some areas require further reinforcement. She has a good grasp of basic structures, such as those used to describe age, gender, and clean/dirty or dry/wet concepts, which she can repeat and apply with support. She understands and can follow instructions for activities like dressing Emma or completing worksheets, and she has shown progress in pairing actions with the correct rooms. However, Chloé struggles with vocabulary retention, particularly colours and clothing items, often needing the teacher to remind her of words or structures. While she can repeat the story and participate in storytelling with help, she does not yet know it independently. Her comprehension of the "Emma is wearing..." activity is improving, but she benefits from additional prompts. Chloé’s strengths lie in her ability to follow routines and her enthusiasm for hands-on tasks, while her primary weakness is vocabulary recall, which would improve with more consistent exposure and practice.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé is a playful and sociable student who generally integrates well with her group. She responds positively to the class’s traffic light system, calming down when reminded to do so, and she treats her classmates and teacher with respect. However, she can be easily distracted, especially when tired or when the group is lively, and she sometimes requires individual reminders to pay attention during instructions. Chloé’s mood occasionally affects her focus—she may ask repetitive questions (e.g., "quoi quoi quoi") or seek reassurance about tasks she already understands. With guidance, she quickly refocuses and participates appropriately. She shares materials and collaborates well in group activities, though she may need encouragement to work independently with new content.

Final comment

Chloé has made steady progress this term, particularly in her ability to use familiar structures and follow classroom routines. Her participation is strongest in activities that involve movement or visual aids, such as dressing Emma or placing tattoos on body parts, where she demonstrates understanding with a little help. While her vocabulary retention—especially for colours and clothing—remains a challenge, her willingness to engage and her cooperative attitude are commendable. To build on her strengths, it would be beneficial for Chloé to review vocabulary regularly, perhaps through games or songs, to reinforce her memory of key terms. With continued practice and focus, she will gain confidence in using new words and structures independently. It has been a pleasure to see her grow more comfortable and enthusiastic in class!

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples "}]

response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Class: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé usually comes to class in a variable mood, sometimes tired or distracted, but she can also be in a very good mood and eager to participate. She shows willingness to learn, especially when activities involve repetition or familiar structures, such as telling her age and gender, which she does almost always using the complete structure. Chloé enjoys interactive activities like the "what's missing" game and dressing Emma, where she actively engages with the material. However, she occasionally holds back when new vocabulary or concepts are introduced, such as colours or clothing items, requiring more guidance than her peers. Chloé is generally cooperative with her classmates and teacher, though she sometimes needs reminders to focus, particularly when asking repetitive questions or when the group is playful and loud.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:

Chloé’s English knowledge is developing, though some areas require further reinforcement. She has a good grasp of basic structures, such as those used to describe age, gender, and clean/dirty or dry/wet concepts, which she can repeat and apply with support. She understands and can follow instructions for activities like dressing Emma or completing worksheets, and she has shown progress in pairing actions with the correct rooms. However, Chloé struggles with vocabulary retention, particularly colours and clothing items, often needing the teacher to remind her of words or structures. While she can repeat the story and participate in storytelling with help, she does not yet know it independently. Her comprehension of the "Emma is wearing..." activity is improving, but she benefits from additional prompts. Chloé’s strengths lie in her ability to follow routines and her enthusiasm for hands-on tasks, while her primary weakness is vocabulary recall, which would improve with more consistent exposure and practice.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé is a playful and sociable student who generally integrates well with her group. She responds positively to the class’s traffic light system, calming down when reminded to do so, and she treats her classmates and teacher with respect. However, she can be easily distracted, especially when tired or when the group is lively, and she sometimes requires individual reminders to pay attention during instructions. Chloé’s mood occasionally affects her focus—she may ask repetitive questions (e.g., "quoi quoi quoi") or seek reassurance about tasks she already understands. With guidance, she quickly refocuses and participates appropriately. She shares materials and collaborates well in group activities, though she may need encouragement to work independently with new content.

Final comment

Chloé has made steady progress this term, particularly in her ability to use familiar structures and follow classroom routines. Her participation is strongest in activities that involve movement or visual aids, such as dressing Emma or placing tattoos on body parts, where she demonstrates understanding with a little help. While her vocabulary retention—especially for colours and clothing—remains a challenge, her willingness to engage and her cooperative attitude are commendable. To build on her strengths, it would be beneficial for Chloé to review vocabulary regularly, perhaps through games or songs, to reinforce her memory of key terms. With continued practice and focus, she will gain confidence in using new words and structures independently. It has been a pleasure to see her grow more comfortable and enthusiastic in class!

```

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé
Teacher: [Not specified in notes]
Level: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:
Chloé usually participates in class activities with enthusiasm. She enjoys the "What's missing" activity and the worksheet tasks. She is cooperative with her classmates and teacher, though she sometimes asks repetitive questions when she already knows the answer. Chloé shows willingness to engage in new activities, such as placing tattoos on body parts and using counters for bingo. She can be playful and loud at times but responds well to reminders to refocus.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is developing. She can tell her age and gender using the complete structure and understands concepts like clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can pair actions with rooms and repeat new vocabulary and structures. However, she struggles with colours and clothing vocabulary, often requiring teacher assistance. She partially understands the Numbers songs and needs reminders for full structures during activities like "Emma is wearing...". Her comprehension of the story varies, as she sometimes knows it well and other times needs significant help.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is generally well-behaved and integrates well with the group. She can be distracted and tired, particularly when instructions are given, and sometimes asks repetitive questions. However, she responds to reminders and stops when asked. She treats her classmates and teacher with respect, though her repetitive questioning briefly annoyed a peer. The group as a whole was playful and loud at times but calmed down with reminders.

---

Final comment

Chloé has shown progress in her participation and understanding of classroom activities. She engages well in tasks she enjoys and is improving in her use of structures, though she still needs support with vocabulary, particularly colours and clothing. Her behaviour is generally positive, though she occasionally needs reminders to stay focused. With continued practice, especially in areas where she struggles, Chloé can further develop her English skills. It is recommended to reinforce vocabulary at home to build confidence in these areas.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé

Level: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé usually comes to class in a very good mood. At the beginning of the lesson, the group was a bit loud and needed the traffic light, but after that they participated super well. Chloé liked the "what's missing" activity and was excited to hear more about the new story tattoo. When she needed to be reminded not to keep asking where things should go, she stopped.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Chloé's English knowledge is good. She can repeat the structures well and understands the "Emma is wearing..." activity. She could pair up the correct actions in the correct rooms, and she could also repeat the new vocabulary words and the new structures. She could tell her age and her gender, almost always using the complete structure, and she could pair up Emma's dirty and clean body parts. She did the worksheet activities very well and counted super well. She also understood the clean and dirty concept, as well as dry and wet. However, she did not know the colours or the clothes, did not know the story at all on one occasion and needed help to tell it, and she still needs support with the clothing items vocabulary. She partially understood the Numbers songs.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé was in a very good mood. She was sometimes a bit loud at the beginning and could be distracted or tired, but she could calm down when reminded. She behaved quite well and liked the new story tattoo. She was excited to hear more, could place the tattoos on the right body part with a little help, and could also place stickers on her body parts.

Final comment

Chloé participated super well once the traffic light was in place, and she was able to repeat the new vocabulary words and structures. She could tell her age and her gender, and she did the worksheet activities and counting very well. She still needs support with the story, the colours and the clothing vocabulary, but she responds well when reminded.

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the sections Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples

response.output:
Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name: Chloé

Level: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment:

Chloé usually comes to class in a very good mood. At the beginning of the lesson, the group was a bit loud and needed the traffic light, but after that they participated super well. Chloé liked the "what's missing" activity and was excited to hear more about the new story tattoo. When she needed to be reminded not to keep asking where things should go, she stopped.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:

Chloé's English knowledge is good. She can repeat the structures well and understands the "Emma is wearing..." activity. She could pair up the correct actions in the correct rooms, and she could also repeat the new vocabulary words and the new structures. She could tell her age and her gender, almost always using the complete structure, and she could pair up Emma's dirty and clean body parts. She did the worksheet activities very well and counted super well. She also understood the clean and dirty concept, as well as dry and wet. However, she did not know the colours or the clothes, did not know the story at all on one occasion and needed help to tell it, and she still needs support with the clothing items vocabulary. She partially understood the Numbers songs.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Chloé was in a very good mood. She was sometimes a bit loud at the beginning and could be distracted or tired, but she could calm down when reminded. She behaved quite well and liked the new story tattoo. She was excited to hear more, could place the tattoos on the right body part with a little help, and could also place stickers on her body parts.

Final comment

Chloé participated super well once the traffic light was in place, and she was able to repeat the new vocabulary words and structures. She could tell her age and her gender, and she did the worksheet activities and counting very well. She still needs support with the story, the colours and the clothing vocabulary, but she responds well when reminded.

Minor issues include some slightly broad or inferred statements such as being 'cooperative' and 'respecting classmates and teacher', and the Learning assessment of 'Good' may be somewhat generous compared with the notes and reference benchmark, where vocabulary gaps and teacher support suggest 'Satisfactory'. Overall, it meets the rubric well with small grounding and calibration weaknesses.

Name: Chloé

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good
Comment:
Chloé participates in classroom activities with enthusiasm. She can tell her age and gender using complete structures and engages in activities such as dressing Emma, completing worksheets, and counting. She enjoys tasks like the "what's missing" activity and can repeat structures well. However, she sometimes becomes distracted and tired, and initially asked repetitive questions, though she stopped when reminded.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good
Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge has shown improvement throughout the term. Initially, she did not know the colours or clothes but has since been able to participate in activities involving these concepts. She can tell her age and gender using complete structures, pair up actions with rooms, and repeat new vocabulary and structures. She can count well and understand corresponding colours and clothing items on bingo cards. However, she sometimes needs reminders to use full structures and has partial understanding of some activities like the Numbers songs.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good
Comment:
Chloé generally behaves well in class. She is usually cooperative and respects her classmates and teacher. However, she can be distracted and tired at times, and initially asked repetitive questions. She responds well to reminders about classroom rules and calms down when instructed. She was in a very good mood during one session and actively participated in group activities.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown steady progress in her English learning and participation. She enjoys classroom activities and engages positively with her peers and teacher. While she sometimes struggles with focus and requires reminders to use full structures, her willingness to participate and improve is evident. It is recommended to continue practising vocabulary and structures at home to reinforce her learning.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. This section should comment on whether the student shows a positive attitude towards different activities conducted in the classroom: ▪ How does the student come into the classroom? ▪ Do they show willingness to learn? ▪ Are they open to doing new activities? ▪ Are they cooperative with the rest of the group and with the teacher? o Participation: definition: involvement in an event, act or activity. Parents must be informed whether their child participates in the activities that are carried out in the classroom and whether or not they put previously-acquired knowledge, as well as language currently being worked on, into practice. ▪ Outline the degree of participation based on what the student views as being important. For example, he/she is very competitive and always wants to win. He/she gets cross when they don't win. He/she always wants to come first. ▪ Another noteworthy aspect is whether the student holds back when new material is introduced, and whether they take part to a lesser degree than when internalised knowledge is elicited in class. BEHAVIOUR: definition: the way in which a person behaves in a specific or general situation. o Note whether the student is integrated within the group or whether they are easily distracted or unable to focus. o Point out, if necessary, how a student is reacting in light of situations that have had to be tackled with a specific plan. o Explain whether the environment or general mood of the group, or the student's particular mood, affects the student's behaviour. Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the sections Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Chloé
  • Teacher: [Teacher]
  • Level: Emma Saturday 2025/2026

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good

Comment:
Chloé usually comes to class in a good mood and is generally willing to take part in the activities proposed. She is open to new tasks and, once reminded of the classroom routine, she joins in with enthusiasm. At the beginning of some sessions, she could be a little loud or repeatedly ask for confirmation, but she usually settles well when given clear guidance. Chloé is cooperative with the teacher and with her classmates, and she especially enjoys hands-on activities such as worksheet work, bingo, cards and dressing Emma.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Satisfactory

Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge is satisfactory. She can repeat the new structures well and often understands the language being worked on in class. This term, she worked on colours, clothes, body parts, actions, rooms and places, as well as the concepts of clean/dirty and dry/wet. She can usually tell her age and gender and, on several occasions, used the complete structure correctly. She was also able to pair actions with the correct rooms and take part in the story-time activities. However, she still needs more practice with some vocabulary items, especially clothes and colours, and she sometimes needs the teacher's help to remember or apply the full structure. With more regular revision, she will continue to gain confidence and accuracy.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:
Chloé is a lively and generally well-integrated student. She can be a little distracted or impatient at times, especially when she is tired or when the group is especially active, but she responds well to reminders and class management strategies. Once the rules are clear, she behaves appropriately and stays focused on the task. She usually treats the material carefully and works well with the rest of the group. Her attitude in class is positive and she is able to adapt well to the pace of the lesson.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown a positive attitude towards the classroom routines and has taken part well in the activities. She is able to understand and repeat a good part of the language worked on in class, and she is becoming more confident when using complete structures. She still needs to keep reinforcing some vocabulary, especially clothes and colours, but she is making good progress. Well done, Chloé! It is recommended to keep listening to the audio tracks and revising the vocabulary at home in order to consolidate the language learnt during the term.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus a Final comment, as shown in the reference. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The report should synthesise observations from multiple notes into a cohesive narrative. IMPORTANT: Do not invent Data fields (Absences, Frequency of use, Teacher name, Oral Test Mark, or other numerical/structured metadata) — only include facts traceable to the source notes. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations, no fabricated marks, absences counts, or audio-frequency values) - synthesis: observations from multiple notes woven together, not a note-by-note list - tone: warm, encouraging, matches Emma-level examples

Objectives and content of the Emma course, 3rd term --- Term report (3rd term) Data Name: Camille Martin Teacher: Christine Blois Level: Emma 2024/2025 Absences: 2 Frequency of use (audio): Insufficient Participation/Motivation Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Very good Comment: Camille comes to class in a good mood and a smile on her face. She participates enthusiastically in all the activities. Camille truly loves story time. She likes reciting the story with her classmates and her teacher. Camille also really enjoys the "Numbers song", the "Can you please...?", the "Happy face/sad face card game" and the "Where is Emma's tattoo?" activities. Camille loves singing along to the songs and she also shows a real interest in the worksheet activities. Camille is always cooperative with her teacher and her classmates. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Good Comment: Camille's English knowledge is good. She knows a great amount of vocabulary and structures. She can often use them in sentences, sometimes requiring the teacher's help to do so. This term, she specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place (e.g.: sleep in the bedroom, eat in the kitchen, etc.). She has also reinforced her knowledge of the shapes, the colours, the moods, the body parts and the food items. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Camille is a joyful, attentive, friendly and bright student. She treats her classmates and her teacher with respect and care. She listens attentively in class and usually stays focused during the lesson, seldom being distracted by her classmates. On a few occasions, she jokingly kept the material instead of giving it back to the teacher, but usually, gives it back directly when asked. Moreover, she takes good care of the material and always shares it with her classmates. Marks Oral Test Mark: 7.20 Good. Camille can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct. The decrease in her audio listening frequency might explain her good result, instead of a very good one! The teacher still wants to congratulate Camille on her good result! Keep up the good work! --- Final comment At the beginning of the school year, Camille was a very quiet and shy student. Thanks to her classmates’ friendliness and her easy-going attitude, she came out of her shell and has felt comfortable in the class ever since. It has truly been a joy to have Camille in class, thanks to her smiling and her good-humoured attitude. Furthermore, she has also made progress in her English learning, developing both her memory and her comprehension skills. It is recommended to listen to the audio tracks during the summer holidays, in order to keep in mind the vocabulary and structures, learnt throughout the school year. ### Example 2: Informes 3r trimestre Luis Moreau Very Good Emma.pdf Kids&Us. He also seemed to really enjoy the "Can you please...?", the "Happy face/sad face card game", the "Where can Emma...?" and the "Where can you...?" activities. Luis is always cooperative with his classmates and his teacher. Learning process Assessment (Learning process): Very good Comment: Luis' English knowledge is very good. He knows a great amount of vocabulary and structures. He can often use them in sentences, sometimes requiring the teacher's help to do so. This term, he specifically learnt the different actions and the different rooms/places in which those actions take place (e.g. sleep in the bedroom, eat in the kitchen, etc.). He has also reinforced his knowledge of the shapes, the colours, the moods, the body parts and the food items. Behaviour Assessment (Behaviour): Very good Comment: Luis is a composed, gentle, friendly and attentive student. He always treats his classmates and his teacher with respect and care. He is seldom distracted by his classmates or external elements. He usually stays focused and listens attentively, following the teacher's instructions very well. Luis, always treats the material with care, gives the material back directly when asked, and shares it with his classmates. Marks Oral Test Mark: 8.40 Very good. Luis can understand the question and makes an effort to use a complete structure in the answer, even if it is not always the correct one. Congratulations on your very good result, Luis! We're very proud of you! --- Final comment Throughout the year, Luis has opened up and has seemed to really feel more comfortable with his classmates. It is a real joy to have Luis in class, thanks to his diligent and mindful attitude, as well as his friendliness. Moreover, Luis kept developing both his memory and comprehension skills. His oral test results have been in constant progress. Well done, Luis! Furthermore, in order to keep in mind the vocabulary and structures learnt throughout the school year, it is recommended to listen to the audio track during the summer holidays. ### Example 3: Informes 3r trimestre Rayan Bernard de Fortemps Acceptable Emma.pdf Kids&Us. However, he really enjoyed the "Can you please...?", the "Happy face/sad face card game", the "Where is Emma's tattoo?", the "Where can Emma...?" and the "Where can you...?" activities. He was able to understand what was expected of him during the activities and the meaning of the words used in the activities. Keep up the good work, Rayan! --- Final comment Throughout the school year and especially this term, Rayan’s behaviour has really improved. He still needs to move around and explore his surroundings, but he does so in a calmer way. He has been getting better at listening to the teacher and staying more focused. response.output:
```html

Data

Name: Chloé
Level: Emma Saturday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good
Comment:
Chloé participates in classroom activities with enthusiasm. She can tell her age and gender using complete structures and engages in activities such as dressing Emma, completing worksheets, and counting. She enjoys tasks like the "what's missing" activity and can repeat structures well. However, she sometimes becomes distracted and tired, and initially asked repetitive questions, though she stopped when reminded.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good
Comment:
Chloé's English knowledge has shown improvement throughout the term. Initially, she did not know the colours or clothes but has since been able to participate in activities involving these concepts. She can tell her age and gender using complete structures, pair up actions with rooms, and repeat new vocabulary and structures. She can count well and understand corresponding colours and clothing items on bingo cards. However, she sometimes needs reminders to use full structures and has partial understanding of some activities like the Numbers songs.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good
Comment:
Chloé generally behaves well in class. She is usually cooperative and respects her classmates and teacher. However, she can be distracted and tired at times, and initially asked repetitive questions. She responds well to reminders about classroom rules and calms down when instructed. She was in a very good mood during one session and actively participated in group activities.

Final comment

Throughout the term, Chloé has shown steady progress in her English learning and participation. She enjoys classroom activities and engages positively with her peers and teacher. While she sometimes struggles with focus and requires reminders to use full structures, her willingness to participate and improve is evident. It is recommended to continue practising vocabulary and structures at home to reinforce her learning.

```

Data

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation):   Good

Comment:
Mila generally shows a happy and willing attitude in class, particularly when activities align with her preferences. She eagerly participates in action-based tasks and enjoys activities like A present for me, Dice Game, and Family Bingo. However, she sometimes closes up or withdraws when she does not get her way, such as during the stamp activity or when denied the blu-tack. Her motivation fluctuates depending on the task, but she demonstrates strong engagement when the material is familiar or enjoyable.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process):   Very good

Comment:
Mila has a strong grasp of the vocabulary, structures, songs, and gestures covered in class. She can retell the story with minimal assistance and combines mood and action phrases effectively. She understands and uses new vocabulary, such as clothing and color terms (e.g., white, black), and performs well in weather and clothing combination activities. While she occasionally needs support with specific words like "taking off" and "putting on," her overall comprehension and application of the material are impressive.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour):   Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila is generally well-integrated into the group and contributes positively when engaged. However, she sometimes struggles with emotional regulation, such as laying down or turning away during story time when upset about not seeing the Big Book. She also refused to participate in the True or False activity and Dice Game after a disagreement. Her mood can affect her focus, but she often recovers and rejoins activities, as seen when she participated after initially withdrawing due to a headache.

________________________________________________________________________________
Mila has shown strong progress in her understanding of vocabulary, structures, and story content. Her enthusiasm for certain activities is evident, and she excels when the material is familiar. With continued practice and emotional support, Mila has the potential to thrive even more in her learning journey.

Is the group more easily distracted than normal? Is the student especially tired on rainy days, after public holidays, etc.? 14 LEARNING: definition: the process of studying, practising or experiencing whereby an individual or a group acquires specific knowledge, particularly knowledge required for learning an art form or profession. o Use of structures worked on during the story o Level of learning objectives achieved o Strengths o Weaknesses, together with a plan for improvement and commitment on behalf of the school to work on this aspect. Avoid repetitions in the same section. Keep an encouraging and positive tone Reference report (a known-good output for the same notes — use as a quality benchmark, student name may differ):

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored "}]

response.output:
Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

Name:             Mila
Teacher:           Oliver Thursday
Level:             Oliver 2024/2025

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation):   Good

Comment:
Mila generally shows a happy and willing attitude in class, particularly when activities align with her preferences. She eagerly participates in action-based tasks and enjoys activities like A present for me, Dice Game, and Family Bingo. However, she sometimes closes up or withdraws when she does not get her way, such as during the stamp activity or when denied the blu-tack. Her motivation fluctuates depending on the task, but she demonstrates strong engagement when the material is familiar or enjoyable.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process):   Very good

Comment:
Mila has a strong grasp of the vocabulary, structures, songs, and gestures covered in class. She can retell the story with minimal assistance and combines mood and action phrases effectively. She understands and uses new vocabulary, such as clothing and color terms (e.g., white, black), and performs well in weather and clothing combination activities. While she occasionally needs support with specific words like "taking off" and "putting on," her overall comprehension and application of the material are impressive.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour):   Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila is generally well-integrated into the group and contributes positively when engaged. However, she sometimes struggles with emotional regulation, such as laying down or turning away during story time when upset about not seeing the Big Book. She also refused to participate in the True or False activity and Dice Game after a disagreement. Her mood can affect her focus, but she often recovers and rejoins activities, as seen when she participated after initially withdrawing due to a headache.

---

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________
Mila has shown strong progress in her understanding of vocabulary, structures, and story content. Her enthusiasm for certain activities is evident, and she excels when the material is familiar. To further her development, it would be beneficial for her to work on managing frustrations and maintaining consistent participation, even in less preferred tasks. With continued practice and emotional support, Mila has the potential to thrive even more in her learning journey.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Class: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila shows a generally positive attitude toward learning, particularly when she feels confident with the material. She arrives in class in a happy mood and demonstrates willingness to participate, especially in activities like the Dice Game, Family Bingo, and A Present for Me, which she enjoys. Her knowledge of vocabulary, structures, and songs allows her to engage actively when she feels secure, and she often repeats phrases and gestures with ease.

However, Mila’s participation fluctuates when things do not go her way. She tends to withdraw or resist activities if she feels frustrated—such as when she couldn’t keep the blu-tack or use the stamp—and may refuse to take part in tasks like the True or False activity. While she recovers well with encouragement, her engagement is stronger when working with familiar content rather than new challenges. Supporting her emotional resilience during setbacks will help her maintain consistent participation.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Mila has demonstrated solid progress in her language acquisition, particularly in vocabulary and story retelling. She knows the structures well and can repeat them accurately, often requiring little to no help during activities. Her ability to combine moods and actions, as well as describe weather and clothing items (including new colors like black and white), reflects her growing confidence with the material. She also excels in pairing sentences with realia items and completing worksheets, showing a strong grasp of the lesson content.

That said, Mila occasionally struggles with specific terms, such as the words for “taking off” and “putting on” during weather routines. When frustrated—like when she couldn’t see the Big Book—she may disengage temporarily, though she quickly re-engages in hands-on tasks like action activities. Continued practice with these targeted areas, along with reinforcement of her already strong skills, will help her build even greater fluency and independence.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila is generally a cooperative member of the group, contributing positively when she feels included and successful. She interacts well with her peers, eagerly helping to hand out materials and participating in collaborative activities. Her behaviour is at its best during engaging, structured tasks, where she follows instructions and demonstrates pride in her work.

However, Mila can become easily frustrated when faced with limits or minor setbacks, such as not getting her way or experiencing physical discomfort (e.g., a headache). These moments may lead her to lay down, turn away, or briefly refuse to participate. While she responds to gentle reminders to refocus, her mood can affect her behaviour—particularly toward the end of longer activities when the class’s energy wanes. Encouraging her to express her feelings constructively and reinforcing routines for transitions will support her emotional regulation and sustained focus.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________
Mila has shown notable strengths this term, particularly in her vocabulary retention, story comprehension, and enthusiasm for interactive activities. Her ability to use structures independently and engage with peers is commendable, though her participation sometimes wavers when faced with frustration. With continued encouragement to manage setbacks and reinforce her already solid skills—especially in areas like weather routines—she is well-positioned to build even greater confidence. Keep up the good work, Mila! A focus on consistency and resilience will help her thrive in the next term.

Data

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Teacher: Thérèse Garnier
  • Level: Oliver Thursday 2025/2026
  • Period: 2026-02-22 to 2026-05-22

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Good

Comment: Mila generally comes to class in a positive mood and shows interest in the activities presented. She is usually willing to participate and can be very engaged when she feels comfortable with the task, especially in action-based activities and games. At times, however, she can become upset when things do not go as she expects, which affects her willingness to continue taking part. With encouragement, she is able to rejoin the class and show good motivation, particularly when she has the opportunity to help or demonstrate what she knows.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment: Mila has shown good understanding of the structures and vocabulary worked on this term. She can repeat the target language well and, in many activities, uses the new language with confidence. Her knowledge of the story has improved notably, and she is able to retell parts of it with little support. She also performs very well in activities involving weather, clothing, and colour vocabulary. At times, she still needs help with some specific words and routines, such as taking off and putting on, and she may need further practice to remain consistent when new language is introduced. Continued repetition and reinforcement will help her consolidate her progress even further.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment: Mila is well integrated in the group and can participate positively in class. On the whole, she behaves appropriately and works well with the rest of the students, especially during practical and familiar activities. However, when she becomes frustrated or does not get her way, she may withdraw, refuse to participate, or show signs of anger, which can affect her focus during the lesson. She sometimes needs reminders to stay engaged and remain seated during story time. When she is calm, she responds well and shows a much more cooperative attitude.

Marks

Oral Test Mark: 8.00    Good. Mila can understand the question and answers either with a single word or with a structure which is only partially correct.

Final comment

Mila has made good progress this term and has shown that she can understand and use a great deal of the language worked on in class. Her ability to repeat structures, recognise vocabulary, and take part in action activities is very promising. With continued encouragement to manage moments of frustration and to stay engaged throughout the whole lesson, she will be able to keep building confidence and continue developing her English skills successfully.

The report must follow the three-section structure (Participation/Motivation, Learning process, Behaviour) plus Marks and a Final comment, as shown in the examples. Each section should include an Assessment rating and a Comment paragraph. The notes include moments of frustration and withdrawal — the report should handle these honestly while maintaining an encouraging tone. Score 1-5 on: - structure: three sections + Marks + Final comment, each with Assessment and Comment - grounding: every statement traceable to notes (no invented observations) - tone: encouraging and positive while honest about challenges - behaviour: frustration and withdrawal handled constructively, not ignored

She knew some vocabulary and could repeat the structures too. She needed help with the "taking off" and "putting on" words during the weather routines. She got upset because she could not see the Big Book during story time, but did not move to be able to see. The class could tell who was missing and name the family member, repeat the correct structure each time, pair up the correct sentence with the weather realia item, and say most of the words, although they sometimes needed a bit of help. They needed to move a lot and seemed to get bored by the end of the story. - 2026-05-14: Mila could tell the story with almost no help. She got upset by the end of class because I did not allow her to keep the blu-tack. She refused to take part in the True or False activity and the Dice Game. - 2026-05-14: Mila came today. She knew most of the vocabulary, structures, songs, gestures, and story. They all loved A present for me, Dice Game, and Family Bingo. - 2026-05-14: Mila could combine the mood and the actions very well. She knew the phrase that went with her weather card, and she knew the story quite well. She could already tell part of it and repeat the rest. They learned how to say what they were wearing, and they learned the color white and the color black today. They could do the weather and clothing item combination very well, and they did the worksheet activity very, very well. They also showed that they knew the color vocabulary, which was great. All of them could do the color vocabulary, and I was really proud of them, and their behavior was relatively great today, so I was really happy with them. - 2026-05-14: Mila already knew the structures and understood how to use them, so she could repeat them very well. She could also use the new vocabulary words and understood how to use them during the activities. ## Task Write a report card narrative for this student based on the notes above. response.output:
```html

Kids&Us.

Objectives and content of the Oliver course, 3rd term

---

Term report (3rd term)

Data

  • Name: Mila
  • Class: Oliver Thursday

Participation/Motivation

Assessment (Participation / Motivation): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila shows a generally positive attitude toward learning, particularly when she feels confident with the material. She arrives in class in a happy mood and demonstrates willingness to participate, especially in activities like the Dice Game, Family Bingo, and A Present for Me, which she enjoys. Her knowledge of vocabulary, structures, and songs allows her to engage actively when she feels secure, and she often repeats phrases and gestures with ease.

However, Mila’s participation fluctuates when things do not go her way. She tends to withdraw or resist activities if she feels frustrated—such as when she couldn’t keep the blu-tack or use the stamp—and may refuse to take part in tasks like the True or False activity. While she recovers well with encouragement, her engagement is stronger when working with familiar content rather than new challenges. Supporting her emotional resilience during setbacks will help her maintain consistent participation.

Learning process

Assessment (Learning process): Good

Comment:
Mila has demonstrated solid progress in her language acquisition, particularly in vocabulary and story retelling. She knows the structures well and can repeat them accurately, often requiring little to no help during activities. Her ability to combine moods and actions, as well as describe weather and clothing items (including new colors like black and white), reflects her growing confidence with the material. She also excels in pairing sentences with realia items and completing worksheets, showing a strong grasp of the lesson content.

That said, Mila occasionally struggles with specific terms, such as the words for “taking off” and “putting on” during weather routines. When frustrated—like when she couldn’t see the Big Book—she may disengage temporarily, though she quickly re-engages in hands-on tasks like action activities. Continued practice with these targeted areas, along with reinforcement of her already strong skills, will help her build even greater fluency and independence.

Behaviour

Assessment (Behaviour): Satisfactory

Comment:
Mila is generally a cooperative member of the group, contributing positively when she feels included and successful. She interacts well with her peers, eagerly helping to hand out materials and participating in collaborative activities. Her behaviour is at its best during engaging, structured tasks, where she follows instructions and demonstrates pride in her work.

However, Mila can become easily frustrated when faced with limits or minor setbacks, such as not getting her way or experiencing physical discomfort (e.g., a headache). These moments may lead her to lay down, turn away, or briefly refuse to participate. While she responds to gentle reminders to refocus, her mood can affect her behaviour—particularly toward the end of longer activities when the class’s energy wanes. Encouraging her to express her feelings constructively and reinforcing routines for transitions will support her emotional regulation and sustained focus.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________
Mila has shown notable strengths this term, particularly in her vocabulary retention, story comprehension, and enthusiasm for interactive activities. Her ability to use structures independently and engage with peers is commendable, though her participation sometimes wavers when faced with frustration. With continued encouragement to manage setbacks and reinforce her already solid skills—especially in areas like weather routines—she is well-positioned to build even greater confidence. Keep up the good work, Mila! A focus on consistency and resilience will help her thrive in the next term.

```

Assessment (Behaviour): Good

Comment:

Mila generally behaves well in class and is integrated within the group. She participates enthusiastically in activities like the present activity and enjoys helping distribute materials. However, she can become upset when things do not go as planned, such as when she cannot see the Big Book or is not allowed to keep blu-tack. These moments of frustration sometimes lead her to lay down or turn her back during story time, though she responds well to reminders to sit properly. Overall, her behaviour improves when she is engaged and happy with the activities.

Final comment

________________________________________________________________________________

Mila has shown commendable progress this term, particularly in her vocabulary acquisition and ability to use structures in context. Her enthusiasm for learning is evident, though she occasionally faces challenges with frustration when things do not go her way. Encouraging her to manage these emotions will help her continue to grow. With continued practice and a positive approach, Mila is well on her way to further developing her language skills. I look forward to seeing her progress in the coming year.
